items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-3. For same-runway departure separation behind a preceding landing aircraft, what must be true before the departure begins takeoff roll?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-3 supports the same-runway rule that the preceding landing aircraft must be clear of the runway before the departure begins takeoff roll.

choices.text: The preceding landing aircraft is clear of the runway.
The preceding landing aircraft has merely crossed the threshold.
The departure has received a wake waiver initiated by the controller.
The aircraft are on converging nonintersecting runways.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-4, FIG 3-9-5, and FIG 3-9-6. Which condition keeps the same-runway or closely parallel runway wake-timing rule in play for successive departures?
items.explanation: These figures are tied to successive departures from the same runway or parallel runways separated by less than 2,500 feet, including the Category I-behind-Category E case on the same runway or a parallel runway separated by less than 700 feet.

choices.text: The aircraft are using the same runway or the specified closely parallel runway geometry.
The aircraft are on opposite courses and estimated to pass in 10 minutes.
The following aircraft is landing on a sea lane.
The aircraft are using unrelated runways whose paths never cross.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-7 and FIG 3-9-8. Why does projected flight-path crossing matter for departures from parallel runways separated by 700 feet or more?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-7 and FIG 3-9-8 illustrate parallel runway cases where projected flight paths cross. The wake-timing rules continue to matter because the runway geometry can still place the succeeding aircraft behind the preceding aircraft's flight path.

choices.text: Projected crossing flight paths determine whether the listed wake-timing rules still apply despite runway separation.
Projected crossing flight paths eliminate every wake-turbulence requirement.
Projected crossing flight paths convert the operation into helicopter simultaneous takeoffs.
Projected crossing flight paths are relevant only to sea-lane arrivals.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-9 and FIG 3-9-10. For a departure using an intersecting runway, which preceding-aircraft states can permit takeoff roll?
items.explanation: For intersecting runway departures, FIG 3-9-9 covers a preceding departure that has passed the intersection or is turning to avert conflict. FIG 3-9-10 covers preceding arrival states such as clear of the landing runway, holding short as instructed, exiting prior to the intersection, turning before the intersection, or having passed it.

choices.text: The preceding aircraft has either passed/avoided the intersection conflict, or the preceding arrival has met one of the listed clear/hold-short/exit/passed-intersection conditions.
The preceding aircraft has merely accepted a takeoff clearance.
The following departure is Category I, regardless of where the preceding aircraft is.
The tower has issued wind with the takeoff clearance.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-11. What makes the LAHSO-related intersecting-runway departure case different from the ordinary preceding-arrival case?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-11 is tied to land-and-hold-short operations: an arriving aircraft has acknowledged a clearance to land and hold short of the intersecting runway or intersecting flight path used by the departure, under the applicable LAHSO order.

choices.text: It requires the arriving aircraft to acknowledge a land-and-hold-short clearance protecting the intersecting runway or flight path.
It applies when the preceding arrival has no landing clearance.
It is only a same-sea-lane floatplane rule.
It removes the need to issue traffic information.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-12 and FIG 3-9-13. When a departure follows a departure or arrival on an intersecting runway and projected flight paths will cross, what is the core wake-turbulence task?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-12 and FIG 3-9-13 support the wake-turbulence interval rule for aircraft taking off behind a departing or landing aircraft on an intersecting runway if flight paths will cross. The required interval depends on the wake categories involved.

choices.text: Apply the appropriate wake-turbulence time interval based on the preceding and following aircraft categories before issuing takeoff clearance.
Ignore wake timing because intersecting runways are never same-path operations.
Use only helicopter visual separation.
Apply sea-lane landmark distances.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-14, FIG 3-9-15, and FIG 3-9-16. In nonintersecting converging runway operations, what must be resolved before a departure begins takeoff roll?
items.explanation: For nonintersecting converging runway departures, the preceding departure must have crossed the departure runway or be turning to avoid conflict, or the preceding arrival must have completed landing roll and protected/passed the projected intersection as described.

choices.text: The preceding aircraft's projected conflict with the departure runway or projected intersection must be resolved by the listed crossing, turning, holding-short, or passing conditions.
The departure may roll once any aircraft is seen on final.
The departure may roll only after both aircraft are airborne helicopters.
The operation is treated as opposite-course en route separation.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-17 and FIG 3-9-18. If an extended centerline crosses a converging runway or its extended centerline within 1 NM of either departure end, what is the default procedural consequence?
items.explanation: When the extended-centerline crossing point is 1 NM or less from either departure end, paragraph 3-9-9 directs controllers to apply the intersecting runway/intersecting flight path provisions unless specified facility aids/directives are being used.

choices.text: Apply intersecting runway/intersecting flight path provisions unless the facility is using specified aids or directives.
Treat the runways as unrelated because they physically do not intersect.
Apply only sea-lane operations.
Apply helicopter simultaneous-takeoff spacing.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-19 and FIG 3-9-20. For a takeoff behind a landing or departing aircraft on a converging runway with projected crossing flight paths, what determines the wake interval?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-19 and FIG 3-9-20 illustrate converging runway wake-turbulence cases. The interval is category-based, such as 3 minutes behind Category A for listed trailing categories and 2 minutes for the specified B/D, C, and E-leading combinations.

choices.text: The wake categories of the preceding and succeeding aircraft when projected flight paths cross.
Only the published runway length.
Only whether the aircraft are IFR.
Only the surface wind direction.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-21 and FIG 3-9-22. When runway geometry prevents an aircraft from fully crossing one runway and holding short of the departure runway before takeoff clearance, what must the clearance include?
items.explanation: The runway/taxiway proximity figures support the exception where the runway to be crossed is stated with the takeoff clearance: CROSS RUNWAY (number), RUNWAY (number) CLEARED FOR TAKEOFF.

choices.text: The runway to be crossed, followed by the departure runway takeoff clearance.
Only the departure runway number, omitting the crossing runway.
Only taxi instructions with no takeoff clearance.
A LAHSO clearance to the departing aircraft.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-2 and FIG 3-10-3. Between sunrise and sunset, a landing aircraft follows another aircraft that has landed and remains on the runway. What changes the required threshold distance?
items.explanation: FIG 3-10-2 and FIG 3-10-3 illustrate same-runway arrival spacing behind a landed aircraft. Category I landing behind Category I or II uses 3,000 feet; Category II landing behind Category I or II uses 4,500 feet.

choices.text: The category of the landing aircraft behind the preceding landed aircraft.
Whether the controller uses the word shortened.
Whether the aircraft is requesting a simulated flameout.
Whether the landing aircraft is on a sea lane.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-4 and FIG 3-10-5. When a landing aircraft follows a departure on the same runway and the airborne departure remains before the runway end, what distance logic applies?
items.explanation: If distances can be determined by suitable landmarks and the departure is airborne, the departure need not have crossed the runway end if the minimum threshold distance exists: 3,000 feet for Category I behind Category I/II, 4,500 feet for Category II behind Category I/II, and 6,000 feet when either is Category III.

choices.text: The required distance from the landing threshold depends on the involved aircraft categories, with 6,000 feet required when either is Category III.
The landing aircraft may cross threshold as soon as the departure starts takeoff roll.
Only a 200-foot helicopter spacing rule applies.
No distance option exists unless both aircraft are helicopters.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-6 and FIG 3-10-7. For an arrival using a runway whose path intersects another aircraft's runway or flight path, what must a preceding departure have done before the arrival crosses the threshold or flight path?
items.explanation: The preceding departure must have passed the intersection or flight path, or be airborne and turning to avert conflict, before the arrival crosses the landing threshold or flight path.

choices.text: Passed the intersection/flight path, or be airborne and turning to avert conflict.
Received taxi instructions only.
Entered a published hold.
Reported over an en route DME fix.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-8 and FIG 3-10-9. Which preceding-arrival condition protects an intersecting runway or intersecting flight path before the next arrival crosses threshold?
items.explanation: A preceding arrival must be clear of the landing runway, have completed landing roll and be holding short of the intersection/flight path, or have passed the intersection/flight path.

choices.text: The preceding arrival is clear, holding short after landing roll, or has passed the intersection/flight path.
The preceding arrival has only been issued traffic information.
The following aircraft is Category I.
The airport has two nonintersecting sea lanes.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-10 and FIG 3-10-11. In LAHSO with simultaneous landings, which aircraft should be restricted if no operational benefit is lost?
items.explanation: For the LAHSO intersecting-runway/intersecting-flight-path case, if simultaneous landings are involved and no operational benefit is lost, restrict the aircraft of the lesser weight category when known.

choices.text: The aircraft of the lesser weight category, if known.
Always the heavier aircraft.
Always the aircraft on the longer runway.
Neither aircraft, because LAHSO removes all restrictions.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-12 and FIG 3-10-13. Which wake-caution scenario requires attention to the arrival flight path crossing behind another aircraft's path?
items.explanation: FIG 3-10-12 covers aircraft landing on a crossing runway behind a departure requiring wake separation when the arrival crosses behind the departure's rotation point. FIG 3-10-13 covers VFR arrivals crossing behind specified arriving aircraft categories.

choices.text: A landing aircraft on a crossing runway whose flight path will cross behind a departure or specified arriving aircraft requiring wake caution.
A same-direction taxi operation with no airborne flight path.
A helicopter hover taxi inside a ramp boundary only.
An en route opposite-course passing report.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-14. What is operationally true about an aircraft conducting an overhead maneuver?
items.explanation: An aircraft conducting an overhead maneuver is VFR, and its IFR flight plan is canceled when it reaches the initial point on the initial approach portion of the maneuver.

choices.text: It is VFR, and the IFR flight plan is canceled at the initial point on the initial approach portion.
It remains IFR until touchdown regardless of the initial point.
It is a sea-lane procedure.
It can never be assigned pattern altitude or break instructions.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-15 and FIG 3-10-17. Before approving SFO/ELP or practice precautionary approaches, which altitude information must be obtained?
items.explanation: For SFO/ELP/practice precautionary approaches, the controller must obtain the high-key altitude or practice precautionary approach maneuvering altitudes of the aircraft concerned before approving the approach.

choices.text: The high-key altitude or practice precautionary approach maneuvering altitudes.
Only the aircraft's filed cruise altitude.
Only the sea-lane threshold distance.
Only the wind direction.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-16. For a straight-in simulated flameout approach, when is low-approach clearance or alternate instruction normally issued?
items.explanation: For straight-in SFO approaches, request a position report and issue low-approach clearance or alternate instructions at the appropriate position on final, normally no closer than 3 miles.

choices.text: At the appropriate position on final, normally no closer than 3 miles.
Before the aircraft reports any final position.
Only after touchdown.
Only when the aircraft is clear of the sea lane.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-11-1 and FIG 3-11-2. Before a helicopter or powered-lift aircraft departs behind another helicopter/powered-lift operation, what must the preceding aircraft have done?
items.explanation: For helicopter/powered-lift departure separation, a preceding departure must have left the takeoff area, or a preceding arrival must have taxied off the landing area.

choices.text: A preceding departure has left the takeoff area, or a preceding arrival has taxied off the landing area.
A preceding arrival has merely called the tower.
The following aircraft is 3 miles from the airport.
The preceding aircraft is established on an overhead maneuver.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-11-3, FIG 3-11-4, and FIG 3-11-5. Before a helicopter or powered-lift aircraft lands behind another helicopter/powered-lift operation, which conditions satisfy arrival separation?
items.explanation: For helicopter/powered-lift arrival separation, a preceding arrival must have come to a stop or taxied off the landing area, or a preceding departure must have left the landing area.

choices.text: The preceding arrival has stopped or taxied off, or the preceding departure has left the landing area.
The preceding aircraft has only been issued landing clearance.
The following aircraft has a 3-minute same-runway wake interval.
The aircraft are on opposite en route courses.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-11-6. What are the two conditions for simultaneous helicopter or powered-lift landings or takeoffs?
items.explanation: Simultaneous helicopter or powered-lift landings or takeoffs require at least 200 feet between landing/takeoff points and courses to be flown that do not conflict.

choices.text: At least 200 feet between landing/takeoff points and nonconflicting courses.
At least 3,000 feet between points and the same course.
Only a pilot request, with no spacing condition.
Only visual separation from a fixed-wing aircraft.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-12-1 and FIG 3-12-2. For same sea-lane departure separation, when can distance by suitable landmarks allow the preceding aircraft to be airborne rather than past the sea-lane end?
items.explanation: For sea-lane departure separation, the other aircraft normally must have crossed the sea-lane end or turned to avert conflict. If suitable landmarks allow distance determination, the other aircraft need only be airborne if the category-based distance minimum exists.

choices.text: When the appropriate category-based landmark distance exists between aircraft.
Whenever the following aircraft is ready for immediate takeoff.
Only when both aircraft are helicopters.
Only when LAHSO has been acknowledged.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-12-3 and FIG 3-12-4. Between sunrise and sunset, what sea-lane arrival distance applies when a Category II aircraft lands behind a Category I or II aircraft that has landed but is still in the sea lane?
items.explanation: For sea-lane arrivals behind a landed aircraft, Category I landing behind Category I or II uses 2,000 feet, while Category II landing behind Category I or II uses 2,500 feet.

choices.text: 2,500 feet from the landing threshold.
1,500 feet from the landing threshold.
3 minutes from takeoff roll.
30 miles from a DME fix.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-12-5 and FIG 3-12-6. A sea-lane arrival follows a departure that is airborne and remains before the end of the sea lane. What controls whether the arrival may cross the landing threshold?
items.explanation: If suitable landmarks permit distance determination and the departing aircraft is airborne, the departure need not have crossed the sea-lane end if the appropriate category-based distance from the landing threshold exists: 1,500 feet when only Category I aircraft are involved, 3,000 feet when either is Category II, and 6,000 feet when either is Category III.

choices.text: The category-based distance from the landing threshold, if suitable landmarks allow distance determination.
Only whether the aircraft are on a paved runway.
Only whether both aircraft are using LAHSO.
Only whether the departing aircraft has called clear of the runway.
